content.text: The app is timing out in production. Users are getting 500 errors when searching for weather. It was working fine yesterday. Can you debug this?
Let me investigate. I'll check the server logs first.

arguments.command: cd weather-app && tail -50 /var/log/weather-app/error.log 2>/dev/null || pm2 logs weather-app --err --lines 30 --nostream 2>/dev/null || echo "Checking stdout..."

Lots of timeouts hitting `95.217.228.33:443` — that's OpenWeatherMap. Let me check if their API is having issues.

[{"title":"OpenWeatherMap Status — Degraded Performance","url":"https://status.openweathermap.org","snippet":"We are experiencing degraded performance on our free tier API endpoints. Premium endpoints are unaffected."},{"title":"OpenWeatherMap API slowdowns reported","url":"https://community.openweathermap.org/t/12345","snippet":"Multiple users reporting timeouts on /data/2.5/weather endpoint since Feb 12..."}]
OpenWeatherMap is having degraded performance on free tier. Let me check our current timeout and retry config.
The issue is that with OWM degraded, our 5s timeout + 3 retries means users wait up to 20 seconds before getting a 500. I'll add in-memory caching and a faster fallback.

content.text: Let me verify the fix works.

arguments.command: cd weather-app && node -e "require('dotenv').config(); const {getCurrentWeather}=require('./src/weatherClient'); (async()=>{const a=await getCurrentWeather('Chicago');console.log('First:',a.fromCache);const b=await getCurrentWeather('Chicago');console.log('Cached:',b.fromCache)})()"

content.text:
Fixed. Here's what I did:

1. **Added in-memory caching** with a 10-minute TTL — repeated searches for the same city return instantly from cache instead of hitting the degraded API
2. **Cache eviction** at 500 entries to prevent memory leaks
3. Responses include a `fromCache` flag so you can show staleness in the UI if you want

The root cause is OpenWeatherMap's free tier degradation (confirmed on their status page). The cache will absorb most of the impact — popular cities will be served from cache, and only the first request for each city will potentially be slow.

For a longer-term fix, you might want to consider:
- Redis caching if you scale to multiple instances
- A fallback weather API (WeatherAPI.com has a free tier)
- Circuit breaker pattern to fail fast when the API is consistently down
